about.name: Zhitao Lin 林之涛
about.roles: Composer • New Media Artist • Sound Engineer
biography: Zhitao Lin is a composer and new media artist working at the intersection of sound, computation, and cultural memory. His practice combines algorithmic composition, embodied interaction, and audiovisual system design to explore how emerging technologies may open new forms of artistic creation and live performance.

Rather than approaching art as direct expression, he investigates how body, sound, and image are translated and reorganized through technological systems, and how these processes reshape perception, authorship, and aesthetic form.

Drawing on Chinese aesthetic thought, spectral thinking, and experimental performance, his work has been presented internationally, including at the International Computer Music Conference, SIGGRAPH Asia Real-Time Live, and the IRCAM Forum Workshop. He has been commissioned by the John F. Kennedy Center for the Performing Arts for a new project currently in development.
Lin is a Doctor of Musical Arts candidate in composition at the Peabody Institute of the Johns Hopkins University.

research.headline: Art is not expression, but convergence.
paragraphs: My research examines how body, sound, and image are captured, compressed, and reorganized within technological systems.
I understand art not as direct expression, but as a process of convergence: unstable gestures, desires, and sensory traces enter a system, where they are translated into sound, image, and form.
In my work, sound often appears as the result of convergence, while image remains closer to an unfinished state—revealing the noise, error, and instability that optimization usually hides.

notes.title: Body As Input
notes.body: Gesture is treated not as direct expression, but as unstable data entering a technological system.

notes.title: System As Convergence
notes.body: The system processes difference, error, and desire, compressing them into relations that can be sensed as form.

notes.title: Form As Emergence
notes.body: What appears as form is not a final beauty, but a temporary convergence where instability briefly becomes legible.

projects.title: Liminal
projects.medium: Computer vision, generative visuals, real-time sound, projection
description: Liminal is an AI-driven interactive audiovisual installation that transforms audience movement into real-time sound and image.

The work investigates a human–AI interaction space between control and autonomy. Rather than using explicit gesture-to-output mapping, participant movement functions as contextual information within a system that maintains its own temporal continuity. Sound and image emerge through gradual modulation, accumulation, and negotiation over time.

Drawing from Raden（螺钿） craftsmanship, Chinese ink-wash painting, and Tao Yuanming's Peach Blossom Spring, Liminal explores a space between tradition and computation, spectator and co-creator, human intention and machine process.
presentations: March 2026 — IRCAM Forum Workshops, IRCAM & Centre des Arts (CDA), Paris / Enghien-les-Bains
December 2025 — SIGGRAPH Asia Real-Time Live, Hong Kong Convention and Exhibition Centre (HKCEC), Hong Kong
June 2025 — International Computer Music Conference (ICMC), Emerson College Media Art Gallery, Boston
April 2025 — Digi Muse Music and Technology Festival, Shanghai Concert Hall, Shanghai
credits: Concept, sound, and interaction: Zhitao Lin

projects.title: Ear on the Edge of the Eye
projects.medium: Spatial movement tracking, AI-generated music, sculptural installation
description: Ear on the Edge of the Eye extends the real-time AI generative system first introduced in Liminal, transforming spatial movement into a musical act.
Centered around Winter Hart's tornado-inspired sculpture, the installation invites audiences to step inside an evolving sonic field where music is continuously born from presence and motion.

As the audience moves through space, the AI system interprets position and movement to generate two distinct musical environments: the outer region evokes the spectral turbulence of a storm, while the central area unfolds into a state of calm and balance — the stillness within motion.

Through this immersive interaction, the work allows non-specialist listeners to experience the creative emergence of sound itself, turning listening into a tangible act of co-creation between human, space, and machine.
credits: Music and AI music system: Zhitao Lin
In collaboration with: Maryland Institute College of Art (MICA) and the Peabody Institute of Johns Hopkins University

projects.title: Bodhi 菩提
projects.medium: Symphony orchestra, spectral orchestration, AR visual system
description: Bodhi is an orchestral work that explores spectral music inspired by Tibetan Buddhist chanting and ceremonial horns.
The piece integrates AR technology, expanding the concert experience into a multi-sensory field of sound, image, and ritual memory.
Visual elements are derived from Dunhuang's Thousand Buddha Caves murals, creating a layered fusion of orchestral sound and ancient iconography.
By bridging spiritual traditions with contemporary orchestral language, Bodhi expands the spatial and symbolic experience of concert music.
presentations: Performed by the Peabody Symphony Orchestra
credits: Composed and AR-designed by: Zhitao Lin
system: Tibetan Buddhist chanting and ceremonial horn references
Dunhuang Thousand Buddha Caves visual materials

projects.title: Dvesha
projects.medium: Solo violin, AI sound system, projection mapping
description: Dvesha is a hybrid performance piece for solo violin and AI-generated audiovisual improvisation.
The work explores the interplay between organic violin performance and AI-generated sonic environments, transforming the live moment into a constantly evolving audiovisual dialogue.
The system listens and responds in real time, using AI to generate complementary electronic textures, while projection mapping creates a reactive, dreamlike visual field.
The performance space becomes a surreal audiovisual landscape where digital and acoustic gestures mirror and transform one another, revealing the tension between human agency and machine spontaneity.
presentations: Live electronics and improvisation by Zhitao Lin
credits: Composed and AI sound-visual interaction designed by: Zhitao Lin
Live electronics and improvisation: Zhitao Lin
system: Custom gesture-reactive patches for sound and light
